metadata.description: Expanded dataset v2: 40 infrastructure project report excerpts for schedule-delay classification
metadata.base: Original 15 cases from dataset_flo + 25 new cases in JICA/ADB report style
changes_from_v1: case_08 relabeled from Uncertain to No Delay (consistency with case_07)
Added 25 new cases covering mixed_signal, scope_change, partial_impact, no_schedule_routine, and additional explicit_delay/stable_progress

label_policy.Delay: Explicit statement of schedule overrun, confirmed delay, or quantified duration excess
label_policy.No Delay: Explicit on-time/early completion, delay with no impact, delay fully recovered, or no schedule information
label_policy.Uncertain: No sufficient schedule evidence, ambiguous context, hypothetical/general references to delay, or genuinely conflicting signals with no resolution

cases.text: The actual project period was longer than planned (ratio against the plan: 124%), but it was proportional to the increase in the length of the targeted roads, i.e., outputs, from planned 300km (approximate) at the appraisal to actual 376.68km (ratio against the plan: 126%)9. Contributing factors behind the longer project period other than the increase in the outputs included, a delay in mobilizing the Special Assistance for Project Implementation by JICA and the interruptions of part of the construction works by the flooding in October 2010 – February 2011 and December 2012 – March 2013. Nevertheless, a delay exceeding the schedule that was revised due to the increase in the outputs was avoided by controlling the implementation schedule.
cases.rationale: Explicit: project period was 124% of plan. Mixed signal: also says revised-schedule delay was avoided. Gold is Delay because the original schedule was exceeded.
key_evidence: project period was longer than planned (ratio against the plan: 124%)

cases.text: At the time of appraisal, the project period was estimated to be from the signing of the loan agreement in September 2001 up to October 2005 (4 years and two months) (50 months). Completion of public engineering works (except for the maintenance period) was planned to be the project completion. When examined at the time of the ex-post evaluation, the construction was completed in June 2012 (10 years and 10 months) (130 months). The project period was 260% longer than initially planned. The executing agency spent longer than expected selecting consultants because of the approval process within the Moroccan government. Moreover, the construction was thought to be delayed because the additional financing required the construction cost to be estimated again, and there was also a project optimization study. Japanese-descended consultants were selected but had frequent communication problems with the executing agency due to the lack of experience of the yen loan project. The situation worsened when a project manager took extended sick leave during the project and there was a need for a replacement. There was also the same problem between contractors for Project (I) and the executing agency.14 In light of the above, the project period was significantly longer than planned.
cases.rationale: Unambiguous: 260% longer than planned.
key_evidence: The project period was 260% longer than initially planned

cases.text: This project constructed an east-to-west arterial road including underwater tunnel traversing the Saigon River in order to increase transportation capacity and improve transportation conditions; thereby contributing to the urban development along the eastern bank of the Saigon River and to the economic development of Ho Chi Minh City, which is Vietnam's largest city and a hub of commerce and industry. Relevance of this project is high because of its confirmed consistency with economic development policies laid out in Vietnam's Socio-economic Development Strategy 2011-2020 and Spatial Development Direction 2010-2020, the need for developing urban transportation infrastructure, and the assistance policy of the Japanese Government. As for efficiency, project outputs were implemented mostly as planned, but project costs exceeded the initial plan due to cost increases caused by higher land acquisition and resettlement costs as well as soil improvement work due to the discovery of soft ground. The project period was significantly longer than the initial plan due to delays in procedures, design and construction. Thus, the efficiency is low.
cases.rationale: Explicit: project period was significantly longer than the initial plan due to delays.
key_evidence: The project period was significantly longer than the initial plan due to delays in procedures, design and construction

cases.text: Tranche 3. At appraisal, the project cost was $150 million; ADB's share was $127 million (84.6%), and the government was $23 million (15.3%). With the addition of DFID cofinancing, the share was revised to 15.3% for the government, 58.5% ($87.8 million) for ADB, and 26.1% ($39.2 million) for DFID. Implementation was delayed because of (i) a change in the scope of works, i.e., from 4 lanes to 6 lanes; (ii) a change in the design of bridge piles due to seismic design provisions; and (iii) uncertain geotechnical conditions encountered during the piling works for one bridge.
cases.rationale: Explicit: 'Implementation was delayed because of...'
key_evidence: Implementation was delayed because of

cases.text: The program is rated less than effective because the target outcomes and outputs designed at appraisal were although achieved, but with delays and through standalone loans. The physical outputs achieved within the MFF period were less than planned. The remaining outputs (M4 sections II and III from standalone loans) were initiated due to the MFF and were completed within 2 years after the MFF period.
cases.rationale: Explicit: 'achieved, but with delays'.
key_evidence: achieved, but with delays

cases.text: The ex-post evaluation confirmed that the project completion was delayed by approximately 28 months beyond the originally planned date. The main factors contributing to the delay included prolonged land acquisition procedures requiring coordination among multiple government agencies, unexpected archaeological findings at two bridge sites necessitating redesign, and a six-month suspension of construction activities during the monsoon season of 2014 due to unusually severe flooding. The executing agency submitted revised implementation schedules on three occasions during the project period.
cases.rationale: Explicit and quantified: 28-month delay with multiple causes enumerated.
key_evidence: project completion was delayed by approximately 28 months

cases.text: At the time of the ex-post evaluation, the actual project period was 84 months against the planned 48 months (ratio of 175%). The delay was primarily attributed to the complexity of resettlement activities, which required the relocation of 1,247 households. Negotiations with affected communities extended over 18 months, during which construction along a 12km section of the alignment was suspended. The contractor also reported difficulties in sourcing qualified operators for the tunnel boring machine, which further contributed to schedule slippage.
cases.rationale: Explicit: 175% of planned duration with enumerated delay causes.
key_evidence: actual project period was 84 months against the planned 48 months (ratio of 175%)

cases.text: The overall project efficiency is rated as low. While the project outputs were largely achieved as planned, the implementation period extended from the originally estimated 5 years to 9 years and 3 months. Procurement delays accounted for approximately 18 months of the overrun, as the initial bid evaluation for the main civil works contract was challenged by an unsuccessful bidder, resulting in a re-tendering process. Construction was also interrupted for approximately 8 months due to a contractual dispute between the main contractor and a key subcontractor regarding payment terms for earthworks.
cases.rationale: Explicit: extended from 5 years to 9 years 3 months with specific delay causes.
key_evidence: implementation period extended from the originally estimated 5 years to 9 years and 3 months

cases.text: The city's major freeway expansion initiative, completed ahead of schedule, added new lanes, upgraded interchanges, and implemented advanced traffic management systems, resulting in reduced congestion and improved traffic flow for commuters.
cases.rationale: Explicit: completed ahead of schedule.
key_evidence: completed ahead of schedule

cases.text: The successful reconstruction of downtown's primary avenues included resurfacing roads, upgrading traffic signals, and adding pedestrian-friendly features, creating a safer and more efficient urban environment for residents and visitors.
cases.rationale: No schedule information. Positive tone, no disruptions mentioned.

cases.text: Last year's bridge rehabilitation project, executed seamlessly, reinforced structural integrity, improved load-bearing capacity, and enhanced safety measures for motorists and pedestrians alike.
cases.rationale: Relabeled from Uncertain to No Delay for consistency with case_07. 'Executed seamlessly' implies no issues.

cases.text: The completion of the city's ring road expansion, ahead of schedule, added new lanes, improved intersections, and reduced travel times, benefiting commuters and enhancing regional connectivity.
cases.rationale: Explicit: ahead of schedule.
key_evidence: ahead of schedule

cases.text: The successful implementation of smart traffic lights and real-time traffic monitoring systems, completed on time, optimized signal timings, reduced wait times, and improved overall traffic management efficiency.
cases.rationale: Explicit: completed on time.
key_evidence: completed on time

cases.text: The ex-post evaluation found that the road improvement project was completed within the planned implementation period of 48 months. All 14 road sections totaling 186km were rehabilitated as specified in the original scope. The contractor mobilized on schedule, and milestone inspections were conducted at quarterly intervals without any notable deviations from the baseline schedule. The executing agency reported that the project was handed over to the provincial road maintenance authority on the originally planned date of March 2019.
cases.rationale: Explicit: completed within planned period, handed over on originally planned date.
key_evidence: completed within the planned implementation period of 48 months
handed over to the provincial road maintenance authority on the originally planned date

cases.text: The project achieved its target outputs ahead of the revised completion date. The construction of 42 bridges and 267km of road was completed by September 2017, approximately four months earlier than the scheduled completion date of January 2018. The executing agency attributed the early completion to favorable weather conditions during the 2016-2017 dry season and effective coordination between the two main civil works contractors. Final inspection confirmed that all works met the specified quality standards.
cases.rationale: Explicit: completed four months early.
key_evidence: completed by September 2017, approximately four months earlier than the scheduled completion date

cases.text: At the time of appraisal, the project period was estimated at 60 months from loan effectiveness. The actual project period from loan effectiveness to the completion of all civil works was 58 months. The project was therefore completed slightly ahead of schedule. Minor procurement delays during the initial phase were offset by accelerated construction during the final year, when the contractor deployed additional equipment and personnel to ensure timely completion.
cases.rationale: Explicit: 58 months actual vs 60 months planned, completed slightly ahead.
key_evidence: actual project period from loan effectiveness to the completion of all civil works was 58 months

cases.text: The provincial road network rehabilitation project was implemented as planned. The project scope covered 8 districts across two provinces, with a total of 312km of roads improved from gravel to paved surface. Construction commenced in April 2015 and was completed in March 2019, consistent with the planned implementation schedule. The quarterly progress reports submitted by the project management unit consistently showed actual progress within 5% of the baseline schedule throughout the implementation period.
cases.rationale: Explicit: completed consistent with planned schedule, progress consistently on track.
key_evidence: completed in March 2019, consistent with the planned implementation schedule

cases.text: The baseline value of all the 39 sections at the time of appraisal (2007) were 40–400 vehicles/day depending on the section, totaling to 3,680 vehicles/day. The target value (1 year after the project completion) in 2013 was 88–804 vehicles/day depending on the section, totaling to 7,739 vehicles/day (increase of 210% in the total value).15 These values were set for the 39 road sections initially targeted at the time of appraisal. These 39 sections can be used to compare the actual measurement value (2017). However, among these sections, the actual value for only 25 sections was available, as shown below (Table 3), due to reasons such as cancellation, suspension, termination, and delay. Also, the sections whose target values are over 80% are with the mark of *.
cases.rationale: 'Delay' refers to reasons for data unavailability, not project schedule.
key_evidence: due to reasons such as cancellation, suspension, termination, and delay

cases.text: At the time of the appraisal for the Rural Road Improvement Project, the Country Assistance Program for Sri Lanka (April 2004) had not yet been prepared. However, the priority areas of Japan's ODA policy to Sri Lanka at that time focused on the two main pillars of "support for the establishment and restoration of peace" and a "support plan with a vision for mid and long- term development". The latter included "institutional reform and support for the development of an economic foundation" 8. Furthermore, JICA' s Country Assistance Strategy for Sri Lanka (2002) included its policy to support the improvement of skills of the executing agencies in planning and maintenance for the development of the provincial/rural road network which was one of the factors that had caused delay in rural development. At the time of the appraisal for the Provincial/Rural Road Development Project (Central Province and Sabaragamuwa Province), the Country Assistance Program for Sri Lanka (April 2004) showed the same priority areas as it had at the time of the appraisal for the Rural Road Improvement Project. JICA's Country Assistance Strategy for Sri Lanka (2008) mentioned the need to "develop infrastructure for mid and long-term economic growth which contributes to the elimination of regional disparities". The aim of this project was to improve the significantly deteriorated surface of provincial/rural roads, and to develop the basic economic infrastructure for daily life. Therefore, it is consistent with the priority areas of Japan's ODA policy to Sri Lanka, the Country Assistance Program for Sri Lanka (April 2004) and with JICA's Country Assistance Strategy for Sri Lanka (2002 and 2008) at the time of the appraisal of two target projects.
cases.rationale: 'Delay in rural development' is a policy background statement, not project schedule.
key_evidence: delay in rural development

cases.text: To arrive at a fair standard of compensation sufficient to cover present the Replacement Value of lost properties, the Property Assessment and Valuation Committee (PAVC) is considered important for each district. Again, to devise a mechanism to dispose of the complaints out of court as per RP, setting up a Grievance Redress Committee (GRC) for each area is also felt indispensable. In order to ensure collective sharing of responsibilities, the PAVC need to be formed with representatives drawn from the EA, DC office, INGO, GRC and EPs including UP Chairman and Women Members of UPs under the administrative order of Ministry of Road, Transports and Bridges which is the appropriate legal authority. Land Acquisition Act of 2017 ensures only statutory land acquisition. This does not guarantee either replacement value of the acquired properties or restoration of income and standards of living of the affected people. Thus in case of projects involving land acquisition and involuntary resettlement, the grievance redress procedure and appeal mechanisms are an important aspect related to R & R of the PAHs. Affected household cannot challenge the land acquisition by Government (which involves a public purpose) in court. Experience shows that the litigation causes unnecessary delay and cost overrun in many projects. Keeping in mind the legal procedures involving such cases, the PMU of SASEC-II will constitute a committee for grievance redress that will provide a forum for people to express to their dissatisfaction over compensation and R & R provisions in RP.
cases.rationale: 'Litigation causes unnecessary delay' is a general observation, not this project's schedule.
key_evidence: litigation causes unnecessary delay and cost overrun in many projects

cases.text: Implementing NGO:The implementing NGO (INGO) will prepare monthly/quarterly reports on the progress of RP Implementation. The INGO office will collect information from the project site and assimilate in the form of monthly progress of RP implementation and adjust the work programs where necessary, in case of delay or problems. An automated MIS will be designed and developed by the INGO to monitor the output indicators at the project field offices and headquarter level.
cases.rationale: 'In case of delay or problems' is hypothetical contingency, not actual delay.
key_evidence: in case of delay or problems

cases.text: Project supervision included joint monitoring missions by ADB, the World Bank, and the Government of Australia.29 Sixteen joint review missions were conducted, with a detailed joint aide memoire prepared each time. Review missions monitored compliance with financial reporting and auditing requirements and followed up regularly with all concerned. The KFSU prepared performance assessment and progress reports every six months; these covered implementation progress, procurement, disbursements, and compliance with safeguards, based on MISE data. Almost all loan covenants were complied with (Appendix 7), with only one covenant involving road sector reform being partly complied with because the Transport Sector Plan was approved in 2016, rather than in 2011 as it was required under the loan agreement. The delay was caused by the government's request to include sea and air transport in the plan. The delay did not significantly impact the project. The Transport Sector Plan includes a road traffic act and other regulations that have been approved by Parliament, but implementation of some reforms (e.g., road transport management and asset maintenance mechanisms) has been delayed pending ongoing discussions within the National Infrastructure Development Steering Committee. Covenants related to audited financial statements have been complied with. No audited project financial statements are overdue, and no financial management issues are pending.
cases.rationale: Delay occurred (Transport Sector Plan 5 years late) but explicitly stated to not significantly impact the project. Labeled Uncertain because the no-impact statement uses 'significantly' — ambiguous.
key_evidence: The delay did not significantly impact the project

cases.text: The project experienced a cumulative delay of approximately 14 months. Construction of the northern bypass section was suspended for 6 months due to a contractual dispute with the main contractor over unit rates for rock excavation. Although the dispute was eventually resolved through arbitration and construction resumed, the contractor was unable to fully recover the lost time. The executing agency deployed a second contractor to accelerate works on the southern section, which was completed on schedule. However, the overall project completion date was pushed from December 2018 to February 2020 due to the unrecovered delay on the northern section.
cases.rationale: Mixed signal: southern section on schedule, but northern section delay was not recovered. Overall project delayed. Gold is Delay because overall completion was pushed.
key_evidence: cumulative delay of approximately 14 months
overall project completion date was pushed from December 2018 to February 2020

cases.text: During the implementation period, the project encountered a 4-month delay in the procurement of specialized bridge construction equipment due to export restrictions imposed by the supplier's country of origin. This delay affected the commencement of bridge works at three locations along the alignment. However, the executing agency reorganized the construction sequence to prioritize road sections that did not require bridge access, and the bridge contractor subsequently accelerated works by operating double shifts. At the time of the ex-post evaluation, it was confirmed that all project components were completed by the originally planned date of June 2020.
cases.rationale: Mixed signal: 4-month procurement delay occurred, but was fully recovered. All components completed by original date. Gold is No Delay.
key_evidence: all project components were completed by the originally planned date of June 2020

cases.text: The mid-term review mission noted that construction progress on Lots 2 and 3 was approximately 3 months behind the baseline schedule, primarily due to the late issuance of right-of-way clearances by the district land office. The executing agency indicated that it was in discussions with the land office to expedite the remaining clearances and expressed confidence that the delay could be recovered through an extended dry-season construction window. No revised implementation schedule had been issued at the time of the review, and the contractor's mobilization plan for the affected sections had not yet been updated.
cases.rationale: Mixed signal: 3-month delay noted, recovery is planned but uncertain. No revised schedule, no confirmation of recovery. Gold is Uncertain because outcome is genuinely unknown.
key_evidence: approximately 3 months behind the baseline schedule
expressed confidence that the delay could be recovered

cases.text: The overall project period was 72 months against the planned 54 months (ratio of 133%). The executing agency reported that 12 months of the delay were attributable to the suspension of works during a period of civil unrest in the project area, and a further 6 months to the re-tendering of the supervision consultant contract after the original consultant's contract was terminated for non-performance. The executing agency noted that the contractor accelerated works during the final phase, reducing what would have been a 24-month delay to 18 months. Despite the acceleration, the project was completed significantly behind schedule.
cases.rationale: Mixed signal: acceleration reduced delay from 24 to 18 months, but still 133% of plan. Gold is Delay because project was still significantly behind.
key_evidence: overall project period was 72 months against the planned 54 months (ratio of 133%)
completed significantly behind schedule

cases.text: Construction of the elevated expressway section was interrupted for approximately 5 weeks when the municipal authority issued a stop-work order pending resolution of a drainage easement encroachment. The contractor demobilized from the affected section and redirected resources to at-grade road works at other locations within the project scope. Following the issuance of an amended easement agreement, construction of the elevated section resumed and was completed within the original contract period. The final completion certificate was issued on the scheduled date of 15 November 2021, confirming that all works were delivered within the contractual timeframe.
cases.rationale: Mixed signal: 5-week interruption occurred, but fully recovered. Completion on scheduled date. Gold is No Delay.
key_evidence: completed within the original contract period
final completion certificate was issued on the scheduled date

cases.text: The supervision consultant's quarterly progress report noted that the contractor had fallen behind on the embankment works for the approach road to the new river crossing, with actual progress at 62% against a planned 78%. The report attributed the shortfall to higher-than-expected groundwater levels requiring additional dewatering, and to intermittent equipment breakdowns. The contractor submitted a recovery plan proposing extended working hours and the deployment of additional compaction equipment. The executing agency was reviewing the recovery plan at the time of the report and had not yet approved it. The report noted that the critical path activity — piling for the bridge abutments — had not yet been affected.
cases.rationale: Mixed signal: behind on embankment (non-critical), recovery plan submitted but not approved, critical path not yet affected. Gold is Uncertain.
key_evidence: actual progress at 62% against a planned 78%
critical path activity — piling for the bridge abutments — had not yet been affected

cases.text: The actual project period was 78 months against the originally planned 48 months. However, this extension was entirely attributable to the addition of three supplementary road sections (totaling 84km) that were incorporated into the project scope through a loan amendment approved in 2015. When measured against the revised implementation schedule that accounted for the scope increase, the project was completed within the adjusted timeframe. The executing agency confirmed that no delays were experienced relative to the revised schedule.
cases.rationale: Scope change: period extended but proportional to scope increase. No delay against revised schedule. Gold is No Delay.
key_evidence: completed within the adjusted timeframe
no delays were experienced relative to the revised schedule

cases.text: The project scope was expanded through a supplementary loan agreement to include the construction of an additional interchange and 6km of connecting road. The revised implementation schedule allocated an additional 18 months for the expanded scope. However, the actual project period exceeded even the revised schedule by 11 months. The executing agency attributed the additional delay to the late approval of detailed engineering designs for the interchange by the national highway authority, and to the discovery of contaminated soil at the interchange site requiring remediation before construction could proceed.
cases.rationale: Scope change with delay: even after adjusting for scope increase, project exceeded revised schedule by 11 months. Gold is Delay.
key_evidence: actual project period exceeded even the revised schedule by 11 months

cases.text: The project scope was revised during implementation to include the rehabilitation of 45km of feeder roads that were not in the original plan. A loan amendment was processed to cover the additional cost, and the implementation schedule was extended by 12 months. At the time of the mid-term review, the original road sections were progressing according to schedule, but the additional feeder road works had not yet commenced due to pending environmental clearances. It was not yet clear whether the feeder road works could be completed within the extended timeframe.
cases.rationale: Scope change: original scope on track, but new scope not started and timeline uncertain. Gold is Uncertain.
key_evidence: additional feeder road works had not yet commenced
not yet clear whether the feeder road works could be completed within the extended timeframe

cases.text: The project outputs were expanded during implementation to include the construction of 12 roadside rest areas and 8 weighbridge stations, which were not part of the original scope. A supplementary agreement extended the loan closing date by 24 months to accommodate the additional works. The original road construction components were completed 3 months ahead of the original schedule, and the additional facilities were completed within the extended period. The executing agency reported that the overall project was delivered within the revised implementation schedule.
cases.rationale: Scope increase with extension, all completed within revised schedule, original scope ahead of original schedule.
key_evidence: original road construction components were completed 3 months ahead
overall project was delivered within the revised implementation schedule

cases.text: The supervision consultant reported that construction of Lot 1 (the northern highway section) experienced a 3-month delay due to the late delivery of precast concrete segments from the fabrication yard. However, the delay affected only a non-critical path activity, and the overall project schedule was not impacted. The critical path activities — foundation works for the interchange structures — proceeded on schedule throughout the reporting period. The project management unit confirmed that the overall project completion date remained unchanged.
cases.rationale: Partial impact: delay on non-critical activity, overall schedule not affected. Gold is No Delay.
key_evidence: overall project schedule was not impacted
overall project completion date remained unchanged

cases.text: The annual performance report noted that procurement of traffic signal equipment for 23 intersections was delayed by approximately 5 months due to the supplier's failure to meet delivery deadlines. The executing agency engaged an alternative supplier for the remaining equipment. Despite this procurement issue, all intersection improvements were completed before the planned commissioning date, as the civil works at the intersection sites had been sequenced to allow for later installation of signal equipment. The project was completed on time.
cases.rationale: Partial impact: procurement delay occurred but absorbed by sequencing. Completed on time. Gold is No Delay.
key_evidence: completed before the planned commissioning date
project was completed on time

cases.text: During implementation, the bridge subcontractor encountered unforeseen geological conditions at the pier locations for Bridge No. 7, requiring a redesign of the pile foundations. The redesign and additional piling works resulted in a 4-month delay to the bridge construction. However, this bridge was not on the critical path, as the road sections on either side of the bridge could be opened to traffic using a temporary bypass. The project completion date was not affected, and the bridge was completed before the defects liability period commenced.
cases.rationale: Partial impact: 4-month delay on non-critical bridge, overall completion unaffected. Gold is No Delay.
key_evidence: project completion date was not affected

cases.text: The mid-term review noted that the resettlement action plan for the southern corridor was running 6 months behind the original schedule due to delays in census verification and compensation disbursement. The review team expressed concern that this could affect the commencement of road construction in the affected sections. However, at the time of the ex-post evaluation, it was confirmed that road construction in the southern corridor had commenced on the revised date and was completed within the overall project period. The executing agency noted that the resettlement delay was absorbed by adjusting the construction sequencing to prioritize sections where land was already available.
cases.rationale: Partial impact: resettlement delay, but absorbed through re-sequencing, completed within overall period.
key_evidence: completed within the overall project period
resettlement delay was absorbed by adjusting the construction sequencing

cases.text: The project management unit submitted its quarterly progress report for the period October–December 2018. During the reporting period, approximately 14,200 cubic meters of earthworks were completed on Section 3, and base course placement commenced on Sections 1 and 2. Material testing results for asphalt mix design were submitted to the supervision consultant for review. The environmental monitoring report for the quarter confirmed compliance with all environmental management plan requirements. A total of 387 workers were deployed across the three active construction sites.
cases.rationale: Routine progress report with no schedule-related information. No delays or disruptions mentioned.

cases.text: The supervision consultant conducted the regular monthly site inspection on 15 March 2020. Subgrade preparation on the northern approach road was in progress, with compaction testing results meeting specification requirements. The contractor had mobilized a second asphalt paving crew for the southern section. Bridge girder fabrication at the off-site yard was reported to be proceeding. Quality assurance documentation for the completed drainage structures was reviewed and found satisfactory. Weather conditions during the month were favorable for construction.
cases.rationale: Routine monthly inspection report. Normal operations described, no schedule assessment.

cases.text: The implementing agency reported that the detailed engineering design for Phase 2 of the highway improvement project was submitted to the ministry for approval on 28 February 2019. The design covers a total of 156km across four provinces and includes 23 bridges and 4 interchanges. The social and environmental impact assessment was completed and disclosed to the public as required under the loan agreement. Procurement documents for the first three construction lots were under preparation by the project management consultant.
cases.rationale: Pre-construction/planning phase report. No schedule performance information.

cases.text: A stakeholder consultation meeting was held on 12 July 2017 at the district administration office to present the proposed alignment and discuss land acquisition requirements with representatives of the affected communities. Sixty-three participants attended, including village heads, women's group representatives, and local government officials. The main concerns raised by participants related to compensation rates for agricultural land and the relocation of a community health center that falls within the proposed right of way. The project management unit committed to addressing these concerns in the updated resettlement action plan.
cases.rationale: Stakeholder consultation record. No schedule information whatsoever.
